12.13: A shadow: 'Fear God and keep his commandments, for this is the whole duty of man.' Ecclesiastes' closing verdict after surveying all human wisdom is the simplest possible statement of the human vocation: the fear of God and covenant obedience. This is the wisdom tradition's answer to vanity — not a philosophical system but a personal relationship with the Creator. Jesus's summary of the law (love God, love neighbor) is the new covenant's positive restatement of what Ecclesiastes reaches as its final conclusion. But Christ does more than restate: he embodies the fear of God and covenant obedience perfectly (Heb 5:7-8: in the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears ... and was heard because of his reverence; although he was a son, he learned obedience through what he suffered), and in his resurrection he breaks the hevel-cycle, proving that labor in the Lord — unlike all labor 'under the sun' — is not in vain (1 Cor 15:58).
12.1: A shadow: remember your Creator in the days of your youth, before the days of trouble come. The 'Creator' here is the unique term bor'echa — the one who formed you — which is the OT ground for worship: your life came from him and returns to him. In the NT this Creator became flesh: 'Through him all things were made' (John 1:3); Christ is the Creator whom Ecclesiastes calls his readers to remember. The urgency — 'before the days of trouble come' — is the NT's 'now is the acceptable time' (2 Cor 6:2).
12.2: A theme: the extended allegory of aging begins — sun, light, moon, and stars grow dark, clouds return after rain. The darkening of the heavens is the image of approaching death and its surrounding sorrows. The NT uses this same cosmic imagery for the crucifixion: 'From the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land until the ninth hour' (Matt 27:45). What the Preacher uses as an image for individual death, the gospel uses as the sign of the death that undoes death; the sun that went dark on Good Friday rises permanently on Easter.
12.3: A theme: the body's decay in the aging allegory — trembling keepers, stooped guardians, stilled grinding women, dimmed windows. The NT does not deny this physical dissolution but addresses it through the resurrection body: 'It is sown in dishonor; it is raised in glory. It is sown in weakness; it is raised in power' (1 Cor 15:43). The Son of God shared fully in this bodily deterioration (Heb 2:14-15: he shared in flesh and blood so that through death he might destroy the power of death), entering the very decay described here in order to reverse it.
12.4: A theme: the street doors shut, grinding falls silent, one rises at a bird's chirp, voices of song grow dim. The withdrawal from life's sounds and activities continues the aging allegory. Christ entered this silence too: his voice was silenced on the cross, his body buried. But the resurrection overturns this silence permanently: 'Death no longer has dominion over him' (Rom 6:9); the voice that grew dim will be heard at the last trumpet (1 Cor 15:52), calling all the silent back to life.
12.5: A theme: fear of heights, the almond tree's white blossoms (aging), the grasshopper's heaviness (appetite fails), desire fails — the person is going to their bet olamo, their eternal home, while the mourners walk the streets. The phrase 'eternal home' is itself significant: the grave is permanent. But Christ transforms what that 'eternal home' is for those who are his: 'In my Father's house are many rooms ... I go to prepare a place for you' (John 14:2). The mourners walking the streets outside Lazarus's tomb heard a voice that overturned their grief (John 11:43-44).
12.6: A shadow: before the silver cord is severed, the golden bowl shattered, the pitcher broken at the spring, the wheel at the well — the moment of death's irreversibility. The multiple images of breakage that cannot be undone make death's finality vivid. Christ's death was this breakage: the cord severed, the bowl shattered (the veil torn, the body broken). But the resurrection undoes the irreversibility: the shattered is restored, not to its former state but to glory (John 20:27: the wounds remain, but the person is alive). Breakage in Christ is not final.
12.7: A direct anticipation: the dust returns to the earth as it was, and the spirit returns to God who gave it. This is Ecclesiastes' clearest statement of the soul's return to its Creator, though it does not specify what that return means. The NT's answer is explicit: Christ 'committed his spirit' into the Father's hands at death (Luke 23:46: Father, into your hands I commit my spirit — citing Ps 31:5), and the resurrection vindicates that this return is not dissolution but the prelude to bodily restoration. The Preacher describes the pattern; the resurrection demonstrates its outcome.
12.8: A theme: vapor of vapors — all is vapor. The frame verse that opens the book (1:2) closes it here, placing the whole inquiry within the hevel-bracket. The NT does not contradict this verdict for life 'under the sun' but provides the answer to it: 'Therefore, my beloved brothers, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain' (1 Cor 15:58). The resurrection is the only event that makes any labor permanently non-vapor; apart from it, Ecclesiastes' verdict stands.
12.9: A revelation of God: the Teacher was wise and imparted knowledge carefully — listened, investigated, arranged proverbs with precision. This portrait of the faithful teacher who transmits wisdom for others' benefit anticipates the incarnate Teacher. Christ taught 'as one who had authority, and not as their scribes' (Matt 7:29); he arranged parables and sayings with precision (Matt 13; the Sermon on the Mount); and the Word became flesh so that what was formerly beyond reach became accessible (John 1:14).
12.10: A revelation of God: the Teacher sought pleasing words, and what he wrote was honest — words of truth. The dual standard — pleasing and true — describes the character of perfect speech, which only Christ fully embodies. Luke 4:22 records both qualities in a single verse: 'all spoke well of him and were amazed at the gracious words that came from his lips.' He is the Truth (John 14:6), and his words accomplish both effects: they are good news and they are exact; they heal and they judge.
12.11: A type: the words of the wise are like goads and like firmly driven nails — and all come from one Shepherd. The image of the singular Shepherd as the ultimate source of all wisdom is striking and points forward. The NT explicitly identifies Christ as the one Shepherd: 'I am the good shepherd ... there will be one flock, one shepherd' (John 10:11, 14, 16). All true wisdom — from the many teachers and books the verse acknowledges — is derived from the one Shepherd who laid down his life for the sheep; the collection of proverbs converges on a person.
12.12: A theme: the endless proliferation of books and the exhaustion of much study. The Preacher places all human learning in proper perspective: the accumulation of texts does not yield life. The NT offers what the many books cannot: the Word himself (John 1:14); 'these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name' (John 20:31). The purpose of writing converges in the gospel; what exhausts in books finds rest in a person.
12.14: A direct anticipation: God will bring every act into judgment — every hidden thing, whether good or evil. This is the clearest eschatological declaration in Ecclesiastes, and the NT fulfills it with specificity: God has fixed the day of judgment and appointed the judge — the risen Christ (Acts 17:31). Paul's 'we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ' (2 Cor 5:10) is the direct NT statement. The 'hidden things' — the secret acts and the inner motives Ecclesiastes could only gesture toward — will be brought to light (1 Cor 4:5: he will bring to light what is hidden in darkness and will expose the motives of the heart).

10.1: A theme: one dead fly in the perfumer's ointment corrupts the whole. The proverbial logic applies to wisdom itself — a single folly can undo an established reputation. The NT develops this as a warning: a little leaven leavens the whole lump (1 Cor 5:6; Gal 5:9). Christ alone is the one whose wisdom is untainted; the one who knew no sin (2 Cor 5:21) is the only teacher whose instruction is not contaminated by the folly it diagnoses.
10.2: A theme: the wise heart inclines to the right, the fool's to the left. This spatial metaphor for moral orientation runs through wisdom literature. The NT locates Christ at the right hand of God (Heb 1:3; Col 3:1), the definitive orientation of all true wisdom. The cross, which appeared as folly and weakness — a thing of the left — was in fact the right hand of God's power (1 Cor 1:24-25).
10.3: A theme: the fool's folly is self-revealing even in ordinary travel — his judgment fails and he broadcasts his folly to everyone. The NT applies the same exposure to those who reject the wisdom of God: what is hidden is made manifest (Luke 8:17). The cross, conversely, exposes the folly of the wise: 'God chose the foolish things of the world to shame the wise' (1 Cor 1:27).
10.4: A revelation of God: composure can defuse even serious offenses against a ruler. The verse reveals a dimension of God's character — his patience and the possibility of de-escalation even when anger is justified. Christ embodies this composure before Pilate: 'He gave him no answer' (John 19:9), and before his accusers he was led as a sheep to slaughter, not opening his mouth (Acts 8:32, citing Isa 53:7). The composure that secures pardon is his.
10.5: A theme: there is a persistent blunder observable in human societies — those in power make disordered appointments, elevating fools and sidelining the capable. The Preacher names it as an evil 'under the sun.' The NT traces this disorder to the fall and its corrupted power structures (Rom 8:20-21; 1 Cor 2:6-8: the rulers of this age who crucified the Lord of glory). All human governance operates within this fallen disorder until Christ's kingdom comes.
10.6: A revelation of God: fools in high positions, the capable in lowly ones. This inversion of proper order is one of the book's recurring social observations. The NT's great reversal — the Magnificat's 'he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate' (Luke 1:52) — is the eschatological answer: the order disrupted by the fall will be restored, and the capable who were assigned lowly places will be exalted in the kingdom of the one who himself took the lowest place (Phil 2:7-11).
10.7: A theme: the servant class riding and the noble class walking — social order inverted. This observation connects to the kingdom's inversion of rank: Christ came 'not to be served but to serve' (Mark 10:45), taking the servant's position himself. The one to whom all service is owed washed his disciples' feet (John 13:1-17); the social inversion the Preacher laments will be finally corrected when every knee bows (Phil 2:10-11).
10.8: A theme: whoever digs a pit may fall in it; whoever breaks through a wall may be bitten. The irony of evil returning upon its author runs through Wisdom literature (Prov 26:27). The cross enacts this irony definitively: those who plotted Christ's death and demanded the curse 'his blood be on us and our children' (Matt 27:25) found the very condemnation they sought to impose turned back; the one who fell into the pit they dug rose out of it (Acts 2:24).
10.9: A theme: labor under fallen conditions carries inherent danger — quarrying and wood-splitting wound the worker. Christ's own labor was not exempt from this cost: he 'learned obedience through what he suffered' (Heb 5:8), and the work of redemption was accomplished through the wounds of the cross (1 Pet 2:24). The danger that attends all work 'under the sun' was borne by the one who entered the world to undo the curse that made it so.
10.10: A theme: wisdom gives the axe its edge and makes effort succeed where brute force fails. The NT redefines this: the wisdom of God is not a technique but a person (1 Cor 1:24). Christ does not overcome through worldly force — 'not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit' (Zech 4:6, echoed in the NT's theology of weakness). The cross, the apparent opposite of 'wisdom making success,' is in fact the power by which God accomplished what the law's brute force could not (Rom 8:3-4).
10.11: A theme: if a serpent bites before it is charmed, the charmer has wasted his effort; words uncontrolled do the same damage. The tongue as a serpent is a wisdom motif developed most extensively in James 3:3-12: the tongue 'is a restless evil, full of deadly poison.' Christ's words are the contrast — controlled, healing, and life-giving (John 6:63: the words I have spoken to you are spirit and life). He tames the serpent whose bite Ecclesiastes fears.
10.12: A revelation of God: the wise person's words earn goodwill while the fool's words devour him. The divine standard of speech — words that heal and build — is embodied in Christ: 'No one ever spoke the way this man speaks' (John 7:46); 'all spoke well of him and were amazed at the gracious words that came from his lips' (Luke 4:22). His words earn goodwill even as they expose what the heart has hid (Luke 2:35).
10.13: A theme: the fool's speech begins with foolishness and ends in wicked madness — a trajectory of deterioration. The NT traces this same downward arc in the rejection of wisdom: Romans 1:21-32 describes how, beginning with the failure to honor God, the mind becomes futile and descends into disorder. Christ is Wisdom calling out from every corner (Prov 1:20-21; Matt 11:19: wisdom is proved right by her deeds), offering the alternative to this trajectory before it reaches its end.
10.14: A revelation of God: the fool multiplies words about what no human can know — the future. This verse places human knowledge of the future firmly in God's sole possession. Christ himself acknowledged this boundary with respect to his own earthly knowledge: 'But about that day or hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father' (Matt 24:36). The Preacher and Jesus agree: presuming to know God's timetable is folly; only the Father knows.
10.15: A theme: the fool's exhausting labor cannot even accomplish the basic task of finding the city. The fruitlessness of effort disconnected from wisdom is the Preacher's recurring observation. Christ's invitation is the direct counterpoint: 'Come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest' (Matt 11:28). The labor that exhausts without arriving is replaced by the yoke that is easy and the burden that is light (Matt 11:29-30), because it is wisdom's own yoke.
10.16: A revelation of God: woe to the land whose king is a boy — immature, self-indulgent — and whose rulers carouse in the morning. The Preacher sets out the divine standard for kingship: maturity, self-discipline, and governance for the people's good. Christ is measured against this standard and found to fulfill it perfectly: the King who fasted forty days (Matt 4:2), who refused to use his power for self-indulgence (Matt 4:3-4), and who served rather than caroused (John 13:1-17).
10.17: A shadow: blessed is the land whose king is of noble character and whose princes eat for strength, not excess. This portrait of the righteous king and ordered court anticipates the kingdom of God. Under Christ's reign — the king of noble character whose every action serves the kingdom's flourishing — the blessing the Preacher describes is realized. The eschatological feast (Rev 19:9: the marriage supper of the Lamb) is the fulfillment of eating 'for strength, not excess': abundance without indulgence in the kingdom that never ends.
10.18: A theme: laziness causes the structural collapse of the house. The image of the leaking roof and sagging rafters is applied in the NT's parable of the talents (Matt 25:14-30) and the warning against neglecting the great salvation (Heb 2:3). Christ himself is the cornerstone who holds the building together (Eph 2:20; 1 Pet 2:6); the household of God built on him does not sag, while every house built on any other foundation eventually falls (Matt 7:24-27).
10.19: A theme: feast for laughter, wine for gladness, money for everything — the Preacher's realistic assessment of creaturely life's goods. These goods are real; the NT does not negate them but transforms them. Christ's first miracle was providing wine at a feast (John 2:1-11); his last meal became the pattern for the church's worship (Luke 22:19-20); and the eschatological banquet (Rev 19:9) is the permanent form of the feasting that 'under the sun' is only temporary.
10.20: A revelation of God: do not curse the king even in your thoughts — a bird may carry your words. All speech is accountable; nothing is hidden from the one who hears. Christ intensifies this principle: 'I tell you that everyone will have to give account on the day of judgment for every empty word they have spoken' (Matt 12:36). The bird of Ecclesiastes becomes the perfect Judge of the NT: not only outward speech but the inner thoughts that produce it will be brought to light (1 Cor 4:5).

11.1: A shadow: cast your bread upon the waters — generous investment without visible return — and you will find it after many days. The image anticipates Christ's own pattern: his death is the bread cast into the waters of death, and the resurrection is its unexpected return. John 12:24 makes the connection explicit: 'unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit.' What the Preacher offers as wisdom, the cross enacts as history.
11.2: A theme: give a portion to seven, even to eight — generous distribution despite uncertainty about what calamity may come. This counsel of generous risk in the face of unknown disaster is fulfilled in God's own action in the gospel: 'He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all — how will he not also, along with him, graciously give us all things?' (Rom 8:32). The liberal distribution of the gospel to all peoples, seven and eight, flows from that ultimate act of costly generosity.
11.3: A revelation of God: natural processes (rain, a falling tree) follow their own logic and cannot be redirected by human preference. The Creator's governance of creation operates with a consistency that does not bend to wishful thinking. Christ appeals to the same logic — 'he causes his sun to rise on the evil and the good, and sends rain on the righteous and the unrighteous' (Matt 5:45) — to ground the call to love enemies: the Creator does not calibrate creation's gifts by human merit, and neither should those who follow him.
11.4: A theme: paralysis through waiting for perfect conditions — watching every wind and cloud — guarantees that neither sowing nor reaping happens. The NT applies this insight to the mission of the church: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations' (Matt 28:19) is a command that overrides the endless waiting for the right moment. Paul's principle — 'whether in season or out of season' (2 Tim 4:2) — is the apostolic form of the Preacher's counsel: sow without waiting for favorable conditions.
11.5: A revelation of God: as you cannot know the path of the wind or the formation of bones in the womb, so you cannot know the work of God who makes everything. The hiddenness of God's creative work is one of Ecclesiastes' recurring themes. Christ's response to this mystery is not to deny it but to offer revelation: 'No one knows the Father except the Son and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal him' (Matt 11:27). The hidden work of God is made visible in Christ, who reveals the Father (John 14:9).
11.6: A theme: sow in the morning and do not rest in the evening — diligent work without knowing which effort will succeed. This describes the life of faith in the absence of guaranteed outcomes. Paul's apostolic labor operates precisely on this principle: 'I planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the growth' (1 Cor 3:6-7). The sower who goes out to sow in Christ's parable (Matt 13:3-9) does not know which ground will receive the seed; he sows everywhere, trusting the harvest to God.
11.7: A revelation of God: light is sweet and it is good for the eyes to see the sun. The Preacher affirms creaturely existence as genuinely good — to be alive and able to see is a gift. Christ claims this imagery for himself: 'I am the light of the world; whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life' (John 8:12). The physical light that the Preacher calls sweet is the image of the uncreated Light that entered the world to give it permanent day.
11.8: A theme: enjoy many years, but remember that the days of darkness will be many — all that comes is vapor. The realistic frame around human joy: death awaits, and all earthly pleasure dissolves into hevel. Christ's resurrection breaks this frame without denying it. Death no longer has dominion over him (Rom 6:9), and for those who are in Christ, the 'days of darkness' are not the final word: 'whoever believes in him will not perish but have eternal life' (John 3:16). The vapor becomes permanent in resurrection.
11.9: A revelation of God: rejoice in your youth — but know that God will bring you into judgment. This is the key verse of the chapter: the summons to enjoy life is qualified by the absolute seriousness of divine accountability. The NT does not soften this but personalizes it: 'God has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed; and of this he has given assurance to all by raising him from the dead' (Acts 17:31). The resurrection is the proof that judgment is not theoretical — it has a face.
11.10: A theme: banish anxiety from your heart; childhood and youth are vapor. The call to set anxiety aside echoes in Christ's own teaching: 'Do not be anxious about your life' (Matt 6:25); 'Cast all your anxiety on him, because he cares for you' (1 Pet 5:7). The Preacher's counsel is grounded in honest realism — youth passes; the NT grounds it in a Person: anxiety about transience is answered not by denial but by the risen Christ who holds the future.

5.1: A shadow: the command to guard one's steps approaching the house of God — to come near to listen rather than to sacrifice carelessly — anticipates the one who replaces the temple entirely (John 2:19–21). Jesus, the true temple and the one mediator (1 Tim 2:5), is the destination that all temple-approach imagery points toward. The caution against the sacrifice of fools is answered in Christ's one sufficient sacrifice (Heb 10:14).
5.2: The vast asymmetry between the creature and God — "God is in heaven and you are on earth; let your words be few" — defines the problem of access that the Incarnation resolves. Christ, who is both in heaven and on earth, the eternal Word who speaks from the Father's side (John 1:18), is the one in whom the creature's words are received and the divine word fully given. The theme of divine transcendence here points toward the need for a mediator.
5.3: The revelation of God: even the governance of words and dreams reflects the order of a God who sees through human noise to the heart. This wisdom teaching is part of the larger OT picture of God's sovereign discernment — which the NT presents as fully expressed in Christ, who knows what is in a person and needs no testimony (John 2:25).
5.4: The theme: vow-keeping before God reflects the covenantal seriousness of the God who holds his people to their word — and who himself keeps every covenant promise. The NT presents this fulfillment in Christ, in whom all the promises of God find their "Yes" (2 Cor 1:20). The instruction not to delay in fulfilling vows points toward the one who fulfilled every obligation perfectly on behalf of a people who could not.
5.5: The theme: better not to make a vow than to vow and not fulfill — a principle of integrity before God. Christ embodies total integrity and the fulfillment of every commitment; in him, the gap between promise and performance that mars human religion is closed.
5.6: The theme: the mouth that leads into sin and invites divine anger names the problem of human speech before God. Christ is the Word who speaks only what the Father gives (John 12:49) and whose words do not bring judgment but life — and whose intercessory work covers the failures of his people's speech before God.
5.7: The theme: "Fear God" — the closing word after surveying the futility of many dreams and words — is the Wisdom tradition's fundamental orientation, one that the NT presents as properly directed toward the one in whom God is fully revealed. The fear of God that begins wisdom (Prov 1:7) is the fear that, in the NT, takes the form of reverent submission to Christ as Lord (Phil 2:10–11).
5.8: The revelation of God: oppression perpetuated through hierarchies of officials — each covered by a higher — is one face of the fallen order that cries out for a judge above all earthly authorities. Christ as the Lord of lords and Judge of the living and dead (Acts 10:42) stands over every human hierarchy and will vindicate the oppressed poor (Jas 5:4).
5.9: The theme: even kings benefit from cultivated land — even the greatest earthly power is bound to the created order and dependent on what the earth yields. This leveling observation participates in the OT wisdom theme of created dependence, which the NT presents as the frame within which Christ the Lord chose to enter as one who ate and drank and was hungry — taking his place within the creaturely order he sustains.
5.10: The theme: the insatiability of the lover of money — never satisfied with wealth — names the disordered desire that Ecclesiastes traces throughout. Christ's teaching names mammon as the rival to God (Matt 6:24), and Paul declares contentment in all circumstances as possible only "through Christ who strengthens me" (Phil 4:13). The vapor of wealth is answered by "true riches" (Luke 16:11).
5.11: The theme: as goods multiply, so do those who consume them — wealth grows a following but not a self. This observation about the futility of accumulation points toward the "better things" (Heb 11:16) that do not multiply consumption but multiply participation in the divine life; Christ promises treasure that cannot be consumed or taken away (Matt 6:20).
5.12: The theme: the sweet sleep of the laborer against the sleepless anxiety of the wealthy restless — wealth that brings no rest names the fallen condition in which human security is placed in the wrong thing. Christ offers the rest that wealth cannot purchase (Matt 11:28–30); the one who trusts him can sleep in peace (Ps 4:8).
5.13: The revelation of God: wealth hoarded at the owner's own expense — a grievous evil observed under the sun — participates in the OT critique of false security in possessions. The NT presents Christ's parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16–21) as the sharp edge of this Ecclesiastes observation, and Paul warns that those who desire to be rich fall into temptation (1 Tim 6:9). Hoarding rather than holding loosely is the diagnostic of a heart not anchored in Christ.
5.14: The theme: riches lost in a bad venture and a son with nothing — the nakedness of failed inheritance. The fathers who cannot pass wealth to their children reflect the universal limitation of earthly inheritance, against which stands Christ who has secured an inheritance "imperishable, undefiled, and unfading" for those born again into his family (1 Pet 1:4).
5.15: A shadow: "As he came from his mother's womb, so he will leave — naked." This most universal of limits — one cannot take anything from this life — is what Christ assumes and transforms. He entered the world in poverty (2 Cor 8:9) and left it on a cross, having taken nothing material. But through his resurrection he carries humanity into permanent participation in the divine life — the one who was naked on the cross now clothes his people in righteousness (Rev 3:18).
5.16: The theme: laboring for the wind — the nihilistic possibility that effort produces nothing lasting — is precisely what the resurrection forecloses. Paul draws on exactly this Ecclesiastes register in 1 Cor 15:58: "Your labor in the Lord is not in vain" — the antidote to laboring for wind is laboring in the one who has overcome death and promises his work will stand.
5.17: The revelation of God: eating in darkness, burdened with sickness, vexation, and anger — life consumed by its own weight — names the groaning condition that Paul names in Romans 8:22. Christ bore this darkness in Gethsemane and on the cross; his resurrection is the first movement of creation's own liberation from this bondage (Rom 8:21).
5.18: The theme: Qohelet's "good and fitting" — to eat and drink and find enjoyment in one's labor as one's God-given portion — is a limited but real good. The NT affirms the goodness of created enjoyment (1 Tim 4:3–4) but locates the permanent version of this gift in Christ; Paul's "whatever you do, do it to the glory of God" (1 Cor 10:31) transforms the portioned enjoyment of Ecclesiastes into participation in the life of the age to come.
5.19: The theme: when God gives someone the capacity to enjoy wealth and labor — "that is a gift of God." The ability to receive creation's goods as gift rather than anxiety is itself a grace, one that the NT anchors in knowing the Giver. Christ, in whom God's generosity is fully expressed (Jas 1:17), is the one in whom his people learn contentment and enjoyment as a form of worship (Phil 4:11–12).
5.20: The theme: God keeping the heart occupied with joy so that one does not brood over fleeting years — the divine gift of present-moment joy. Christ embodies and mediates this joy: "These things I have spoken to you so that my joy may be in you and your joy may be complete" (John 15:11). The brooding over transience that Ecclesiastes diagnoses is answered in the one who says "I am the resurrection and the life" (John 11:25).

6.1: The revelation of God: God's observation of evil widespread among human beings — the opening of the chapter — assumes the divine moral clarity that sees what is wrong in creation. The NT presents Christ as the one through whom this divine judgment becomes redemptive rather than merely condemning (John 3:17).
6.2: The theme: God gives wealth, riches, and honor — yet withholds the capacity to enjoy it, so a stranger devours it. This disconnection between blessing and enjoyment names a specifically theological problem: the good of creation turned to bitter vapor by the absence of the ability to receive it. Christ, the one who is himself the fullness of God's blessing (Eph 1:3), gives his people not only good things but the Spirit who enables them to receive creation's goods with thanksgiving (1 Tim 4:4).
6.3: The theme: the man who cannot fill himself with good things and receives no burial — worse than a stillborn — represents the extreme case of a life without real participation in created goodness. Against this, Christ is the "resurrection and the life" who promises not existence without fullness but abundant life (John 10:10). He is also the one who received proper burial (Matt 27:57–60), and who promises his people a resurrection body.
6.4: The theme: the stillborn comes in vapor and departs in darkness, its name buried in darkness — the limit of a life that never registered. Christ, whose own name is above every name (Phil 2:9), names and knows each of his own; no life in him departs unnamed into darkness (John 10:3).
6.5: The revelation of God: the stillborn finds more rest than the one who lived long without good. The theme of rest — what constitutes true rest — runs through Wisdom literature into Hebrews, where the "rest that remains for the people of God" is entered through Christ, who himself rested on the seventh day (Heb 4:9–10). The rest the stillborn finds by not enduring a vapor-life is the shadow of the eternal rest Christ secures through his finished work.
6.6: The theme: "does not everyone go to the same place in the end?" — the leveling force of death that all human diversity cannot escape. Christ transforms this universal limit: his resurrection means that for his people, "the same place" at the end is not merely death but life out of death (Rom 6:8).
6.7: The theme: all labor toward filling the mouth, yet the appetite is never satisfied — the structural insatiability of the creature that no earthly labor can resolve. Christ as the Bread of Life (John 6:35) answers this: "Whoever comes to me will never be hungry, and whoever believes in me will never be thirsty." The satiation Qohelet cannot find under the sun is given in the one who comes from above.
6.8: The theme: what advantage does the wise person have over the fool in any final sense? Wisdom gives navigational skills but not ultimate escape from the common fate. The NT's answer is that wisdom finds its ultimate advantage in Christ: "God has made him to be for us wisdom" (1 Cor 1:30), and this wisdom leads through death to resurrection, not merely through life more skillfully.
6.9: The theme: better what the eyes can actually see than the restless wandering of desire — a limited commendation of the present moment over restless projection. This theme of presence over projection finds its NT deepening in Christ's command not to worry about tomorrow (Matt 6:34), and Paul's learning of contentment in whatever state he is in (Phil 4:11) through Christ who strengthens.
6.10: The revelation of God: "no one can contend with one far more powerful" — the sovereignty of God over the creature stands firm. Whatever is named is already known and subject to the one who named it (Gen 2:19). The NT presents Christ as the one through whom and for whom all things were created and in whom they hold together (Col 1:16–17) — the one who is "far more powerful" taking on flesh without surrendering his sovereignty.
6.11: The theme: the more words, the more futility — a principle that the prologue of John inverts. The eternal Word, through whom all things were made (John 1:3), enters the world not to multiply futility but to resolve it; he is the Word that does not return empty (Isa 55:11). The futility of human words is healed by the one Word that carries the weight of divine meaning.
6.12: The theme: "who knows what is truly good for a person" and "who can tell anyone what will happen after they are gone?" — the double unknowing of mortal existence. Christ answers both: he is the Way, the Truth, and the Life who defines what is truly good (John 14:6), and he is the first to return from the dead and report what lies beyond (1 Cor 15:20). The two questions Qohelet poses as unanswerable are answered in the resurrection.

7.1: The theme: "a good name is better than fine perfume, and the day of death than the day of birth." The preference for the day of death over the day of birth — counterintuitive wisdom — anticipates the NT's valuation of death transformed by resurrection: Paul echoes this when he says "to die is gain" (Phil 1:21). Jesus' name, poured out like perfume (Song 1:3), is the name above every name, the good name that cannot be taken away (Phil 2:9).
7.2: The theme: it is better to go to the house of mourning than the house of feasting, for death is the end of every person and the living should take it to heart. Jesus goes to both: he weeps at Lazarus' grave (John 11:35) and feasts with sinners (Luke 15:2). His Beatitudes — "Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted" (Matt 5:4) — take up this Ecclesiastes commendation of the house of mourning and add the resurrection comfort Qohelet could not yet see.
7.3: The theme: sorrow is better than laughter, for a sad face does the heart good. The wisdom that suffering produces depth rather than shallowness runs through the Psalms of lament, the Servant Songs, and Hebrews: Jesus is "acquainted with grief" (Isa 53:3) and "learned obedience through what he suffered" (Heb 5:8). The NT does not abolish this wisdom but anchors it in the one whose sorrow was the world's healing.
7.4: The theme: the wise heart is drawn to the house of mourning, the fool's to the house of mirth. Wisdom that refuses to evade grief is the posture of the prophets and, ultimately, of Christ, who does not avoid Jerusalem and the cross but sets his face toward them (Luke 9:51). The Wisdom of God takes the path of mourning willingly.
7.5: The revelation of God: it is better to hear the rebuke of the wise than the song of fools — a Wisdom principle that recurs in Proverbs (Prov 27:6). Christ is the wise teacher whose rebuke — "woe to you" — is better for those who receive it than any flattery; the prophetic tradition of hard truth-telling climaxes in him who speaks the word that judges and saves (Heb 4:12).
7.6: The theme: the crackling of thorns under a pot — the fool's laughter — bright and quick and fruitless. This image of ephemeral noise has its NT counterpart in Paul's "noisy gong or clanging cymbal" (1 Cor 13:1) — the person who has gifts but not love. Love, which Christ embodies and commands, is the substance that outlasts the noise.
7.7: The revelation of God: oppression makes a fool of a wise man; a bribe corrupts the heart — the social forces that undo wisdom. Christ's own trial is the extreme case: oppression and bribes (the thirty pieces of silver) corrupt the judgment of the powerful against the Wise One. His cross is the point where human corruption reaches its nadir and divine wisdom turns it to redemption (1 Cor 1:25).
7.8: The theme: the end of a matter is better than its beginning; patience of spirit is better than pride. Paul's "patient endurance" and Hebrews's "endure to the end" draw on this wisdom principle. Christ himself endured to the end, and the letter to the Hebrews presents his completion — "It is finished" (John 19:30) — as the end-is-better-than-beginning principle taken to its ultimate expression.
7.9: The theme: do not be hasty to become angry, for anger rests in the lap of fools. Christ embodies this in his "slow to anger" character (Exod 34:6 describes the God of whom he is the full expression), and James picks up the Ecclesiastes wisdom directly: "Be slow to speak and slow to become angry" (Jas 1:19). The one who could have called twelve legions of angels (Matt 26:53) chose patience instead.
7.10: The theme: "do not say, 'Why were the former days better than these?'" — the wisdom against nostalgic retreat. The NT does not commend nostalgia for the old covenant but forward movement into the new: Christ is the new thing God is doing (2 Cor 5:17), and the one who looks back after putting his hand to the plow is not fit for the kingdom (Luke 9:62).
7.11: The theme: wisdom like an inheritance is a good thing, benefiting those who see the sun. In the NT, the inheritance is not wisdom as an abstract quality but "the wisdom of God" who is Christ (1 Cor 1:24), the inheritance secured by the Spirit as down payment (Eph 1:14). The "seeing the sun" language resonates with the Johannine light motif: Christ as the light of the world (John 8:12).
7.12: The theme: wisdom and money both protect, but wisdom "preserves the life of its possessor." The one who possesses wisdom-who-is-Christ has not just navigational advantage but life itself: "Whoever finds me finds life and obtains favor from the LORD" (Prov 8:35 — Wisdom speaking). The NT identifies this Wisdom as Christ (Col 2:3, where all treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden in him).
7.13: A shadow: "Consider what God has done — who can straighten what he has made crooked?" This fundamental acknowledgment of divine sovereignty over reality — even crooked reality — anticipates both the NT's recognition of the fallen world and its proclamation that Christ will ultimately make all things new (Rev 21:5). What no human hand can straighten, the one through whom all things were created can restore (Acts 3:21).
7.14: The revelation of God: "God has made both the one and the other" — good times and bad, prosperity and adversity — "so that no one can discover what the future holds." This divine hiddenness of the future is part of the creaturely condition; the NT does not promise foreknowledge but promises the presence of the one who holds the future: "I know the plans I have for you" (Jer 29:11), and in Christ, the one who is the same yesterday, today, and forever (Heb 13:8) is with his people in both the good and the bad.
7.15: The theme: in futile days, the righteous perish in righteousness and the wicked live long — the observed injustice that cries out for resolution. The psalms of lament make this same cry; the NT's answer is the cross and resurrection. Christ, the uniquely righteous one, perishes in his righteousness — and the resurrection is God's vindication that righteousness is not ultimately extinguished but exalted (Phil 2:9). The final judgment will reverse every apparent injustice (Rev 20:12–13).
7.16: The revelation of God: "Do not be excessively righteous — why should you ruin yourself?" Qohelet's pragmatic counsel against hyper-scrupulosity is different from the NT's call to righteousness, but both address the human tendency to seek security through moral performance. The NT's resolution is not moderate righteousness but imputed righteousness: the "righteous" one is not the person who has achieved enough but the one who trusts in Christ (Rom 4:5; Phil 3:9).
7.17: The revelation of God: "Do not be excessively wicked — why die before your time?" The pragmatic boundary against self-destruction through wickedness assumes that death is the consequence of wickedness — a truth that the NT grounds in the deeper reality of sin and death as connected (Rom 6:23). Christ's death is the place where this connection is broken for those who are in him.
7.18: The theme: grasping both wisdom and not-releasing-the-other, "for the one who fears God will avoid both extremes" — the fear of God as the axis that holds wisdom together. The NT presents Christ as the one who "feared God" (Heb 5:7) perfectly, the embodiment of the wisdom that avoids every extreme, and the one who mediates the fear-of-God that is the beginning of wisdom (1 Cor 1:30).
7.19: The theme: wisdom strengthens the wise more than ten rulers — wisdom as practical strength beyond political power. Christ is the one in whom true strength resides, the one who is strong when his followers are weak (2 Cor 12:9–10), and in whom wisdom is not an achievement but a gift (Jas 1:5). Ten rulers cannot match what the Wisdom of God secures for his people.
7.20: Direct: "There is no righteous person on earth who always does good and never sins" — a foundational assertion that Paul quotes directly in Romans 3:10–12 as scriptural proof of universal sinfulness. Christ alone is the exception to this universal: he who knew no sin (2 Cor 5:21) becomes sin for us so that we might become the righteousness of God in him. This verse is one of the OT texts most directly absorbed into the NT's account of why Christ's righteousness is necessary.
7.21: The theme: do not pay attention to everything people say — you may hear your servant cursing you. The wisdom of selective attention to criticism, combined with self-awareness that one has done the same, is the creaturely limitation of moral consistency that v.20 names. Christ, who is spoken against throughout his ministry and trial, hears cursing without retaliating (1 Pet 2:23) — absorbing the full weight of human criticism rather than being destroyed by it.
7.22: The theme: "your own heart knows that you too have cursed others many times" — the self-implicating move of Wisdom that removes any pretense of moral superiority. The NT deepens this in Matthew 7:1–5: the speck and the log; and Paul: "all have sinned" (Rom 3:23). No one can stand in moral judgment; only the one who has never cursed another — Christ — can both absorb criticism and forgive the critics.
7.23: The theme: "I said I will be wise — but wisdom was far from me." The failure of the self-improvement project to secure wisdom is the Ecclesiastes analogue to Paul's "wretched man that I am" in Romans 7. The wisdom that Qohelet cannot capture through effort is the wisdom the NT presents as a person — Christ — who must be received as gift (1 Cor 1:30) rather than achieved by resolve.
7.24: The theme: "whatever exists is far off and utterly deep — who can discover it?" The epistemic limit of the human knower before the depths of reality is resolved only in the NT's claim that God has revealed his deep purposes in Christ: "the deep things of God" are known through the Spirit (1 Cor 2:10), and in Christ "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" (Col 2:3). What is far off and deep is made near in the Incarnation.
7.25: The revelation of God: Qohelet's turning of the mind to understand wisdom and the explanation of things, to know that wickedness is folly and foolishness is madness — the prophetic dimension of the investigation. Christ is the one who performs the definitive diagnosis of human wickedness and folly, not by philosophical analysis but by entering it, absorbing its consequence, and rising above it (1 Cor 1:18–25).
7.26: The theme: the woman whose heart is a trap and hands are chains — "more bitter than death" — functions in Wisdom literature as the personification of deadly folly (Prov 7:27; 9:18). The one who pleases God escapes her. Christ, who pleased God perfectly (Matt 3:17), is the one who was exposed to every trap and snare of the adversary and emerged unbound — and who liberates those caught (Heb 2:14–15).
7.27: The revelation of God: "Here is what I found, says the Preacher, adding one thing to another to find the result" — the investigative process of Wisdom. The NT presents Christ as the one in whom the investigation reaches its end: not the sum of accumulated observations but the one who is himself the Way, the Truth, and the Life (John 14:6).
7.28: The theme: "one man in a thousand I have found, but not one woman among all these" — the failed search for a person of integrity in the context of the Wisdom investigation. The hyperbole serves the larger argument: one in a thousand is not enough; the NT's claim is that the one needed — perfectly righteous, perfectly wise — has been found in Christ (Heb 7:26: "holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners").
7.29: Direct: "God made human beings upright, but they have gone in search of many devices." This verse is the Ecclesiastes account of the Fall — in Wisdom idiom rather than Genesis narrative language, but naming the same reality: original uprightness lost to autonomous human striving. The NT's second Adam, Jesus Christ, reverses the "search for many devices" by doing only what the Father shows him (John 5:19), living the uprightness that Adam abandoned. Romans 5:18–19 is the NT commentary on this verse.

8.1: Direct: "Who is like the wise man? Who else knows the meaning of a matter?" — the rhetorical question that in the OT registers divine incomparability (cf. Mic 7:18 "who is a God like you?") here applied to wisdom itself. Paul identifies Christ as the answer: God has made Christ "to be for us wisdom" (1 Cor 1:30), and in him "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" (Col 2:3). The brightened face that wisdom produces echoes Moses' shining face and ultimately points to Christ, whose face shines with divine glory (2 Cor 3:18; 4:6).
8.2: The theme: keep the king's command, because of your oath before God — the obligation of loyalty sworn before the divine witness. Paul's instruction to submit to governing authorities (Rom 13:1) and Peter's "honor the king" (1 Pet 2:17) carry this Ecclesiastes wisdom into the NT context. Christ himself, the true King, commands loyalty; but the oath before God that undergirds all human authority is ultimately answered in the new covenant in his blood (Luke 22:20).
8.3: The revelation of God: do not hurry from the king's presence; do not persist in a bad cause — the wisdom of measured withdrawal from power. Christ's own navigating of kingly authority — "render to Caesar what is Caesar's" (Matt 22:21) — reflects this Wisdom guidance, and his refusal to persist in a bad cause (he could have called legions of angels but did not — Matt 26:53) is the ultimate expression of measured submission.
8.4: The theme: "the king's word is absolute" — no one says to him "what are you doing?" This characterization of royal authority resonates with the divine sovereignty language of Daniel and Job (Job 9:12; 33:13). Christ, the King of kings (Rev 19:16), has absolute authority — "all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me" (Matt 28:18) — yet he is not an arbitrary monarch but the righteous Judge who has himself submitted to every human authority unto death.
8.5: The theme: the one who obeys the king's command comes to no harm; the wise heart knows the proper time and method. The NT principle of discerning the right time — kairos — is rooted in this kind of wisdom: "making the most of every opportunity because the days are evil" (Eph 5:16). Christ operates with perfect discernment of time: "My hour has not yet come" (John 2:4), and when the hour arrives, he acts with complete authority.
8.6: The revelation of God: every purpose has its proper time and method, though trouble weighs heavily on the human being. The divine ordering of time — which no human can fully know — is made available to God's people through Christ, who is the same yesterday, today, and forever (Heb 13:8), and in whom the mystery of timing is not chaos but providential purpose (Eph 1:9–10: the mystery of the fullness of time to unite all things in Christ).
8.7: The theme: no one knows what is coming, and who can tell how it will come? The epistemic limit of mortal existence before the future is the condition Christ entered. He who "did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped" (Phil 2:6) took on human unknowing — the one through whom all times are created submitted to not-knowing "that day or hour" (Mark 13:32) in his kenotic humility.
8.8: Direct: "No one has power over the life-breath to restrain it, and no one has power over the day of death." Paul quotes this register in 1 Corinthians 15: no one can escape death — "the last enemy" — by natural means. Christ's resurrection is the direct answer to this universal limit: he alone has "the keys of Death and Hades" (Rev 1:18), the first and only one who has had power over the life-breath that all others must relinquish.
8.9: The revelation of God: one person dominating another to their own harm — the cycle of oppressive power. The divine observer noting this oppression is the God who judges between the oppressor and oppressed (Ps 82). Christ enters as the oppressed one (Isa 53:7) and his cross — the ultimate act of domination turned against him — becomes the point of liberation for all oppressed by sin and death (Gal 5:1).
8.10: The theme: the wicked buried, soon forgotten in the city where they did wrong — the futility of an evil name. Against the evil name forgotten, Christ's good name (Eccl 7:1) is the name above every name (Phil 2:9), exalted precisely because he was humiliated. Those who oppose his people — like the wicked here — will be remembered no more, while those in Christ receive his name and are known eternally.
8.11: The revelation of God: "when the sentence for a wrong is not carried out quickly, the human heart is emboldened to do evil" — the psychology of delayed judgment. The NT addresses this directly: "because sentence against an evil deed is not executed speedily, the human heart is fully set to do evil" (Ecc 8:11 stands behind Peter's warning that scoffers will say "Where is the promise of his coming?" — 2 Pet 3:3–4). Christ's return is the execution of the delayed sentence; the patience of God is his giving time for repentance (2 Pet 3:9).
8.12: Direct: "I know that it will go well with those who fear God, who stand in awe before him." This affirmation — that despite the sinner's long life and apparent prosperity, good will come to the God-fearer — is the OT covenant conviction. The NT places this on the far side of the cross: it will go well with those who are in Christ, because he has absorbed the fate of the wicked and secured the fate of the righteous (Rom 8:1, 28). The knowledge that it will go well is now anchored not in general principle but in a specific historical event.
8.13: Direct: "it will not go well with the wicked, and their days will not lengthen like a shadow, because they do not fear God." This is the covenantal declaration of judgment on the wicked. The NT does not soften but deepens it: the wicked who persist in wickedness apart from Christ face judgment (John 3:36; Rev 20:15). The shadow-metaphor — days lengthening like a shadow before disappearing — matches the NT's picture of the brevity of earthly seeming-prosperity against eternal consequence.
8.14: The theme: the futility that occurs on earth — the righteous getting what the wicked deserve and vice versa — the observed injustice that is "futility." Christ experienced this directly: the righteous received the wages of the wicked (crucifixion as a criminal), and the wicked received release (Barabbas). His cross is the extreme case of this futility — and the resurrection is God's refusal to let it be the final word. The ultimate inversion of unjust inversions comes at the last judgment (Matt 25:31–46).
8.15: The theme: Qohelet commends joy — eating, drinking, gladness accompanying work through all the days God has given under the sun. The NT receives this commendation: "whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God" (1 Cor 10:31). Paul's instruction to "rejoice always" (Phil 4:4) and the NT's persistent commendation of gladness in Christ is the fulfillment of Qohelet's limited joy — given full weight by the resurrection and the permanent day beyond the sun.
8.16: The revelation of God: the sleepless toil of those who labor day and night in searching — neither day nor night do they sleep — names the exhaustion of the human epistemic project. Christ's invitation "Come to me, all who are weary and heavy-laden, and I will give you rest" (Matt 11:28) speaks directly to this sleeplessness. The unceasing labor of knowing is answered in the one who says "I am the truth" (John 14:6).
8.17: A shadow: "no one can discover what is done under the sun — no matter how much one labors, they cannot find it out." This is Ecclesiastes' fundamental epistemological limit. The NT's claim is that God has disclosed the mystery hidden for ages — in Christ (Col 1:26–27; Eph 3:3–6). The work of God that no one under the sun could discover has been revealed from above the sun; the mystery is now open in the one who came down from heaven (John 3:13).

9.1: The theme: "the righteous and the wise and all they do are in God's hands" — a statement of divine sovereignty that is the theological axis of Ecclesiastes 9. The NT deepens this: "neither death nor life... nor things to come... will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord" (Rom 8:38–39). The "hands of God" that hold the righteous are, in the NT, the hands pierced for them — the security is personal and covenantal, not merely providential.
9.2: The theme: the same fate comes to everyone — righteous and wicked, clean and unclean, those who sacrifice and those who do not. This leveling force of death is not denied but transcended in the NT: Christ shares the common human fate (Heb 2:14) precisely in order to break it. "Since therefore the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things, that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death" (Heb 2:14).
9.3: A shadow: "this is the evil in everything under the sun: one fate comes to all; moreover, people's hearts are full of evil." The dual problem — universal death and universal sin — is the precise diagnosis that the gospel addresses. Romans 5:12 stands behind this verse: "sin came into the world through one man and death through sin." Christ's coming is the direct response to this double evil: he who knew no sin bears the consequence of the sin-filled hearts, and in his resurrection, the one-fate-for-all becomes participation in his resurrection life (Rom 6:23).
9.4: The theme: "anyone among the living has hope, for a living dog is better off than a dead lion." Hope attached to biological life — the most minimal basis for hope. The NT transforms this: true hope is not merely being alive but being alive in Christ (Col 1:27, "Christ in you, the hope of glory"). The living dog is better than the dead lion because of the possibility of change, conversion, and encounter with Christ — the one who gives "living hope" through his resurrection (1 Pet 1:3).
9.5: The theme: "the living know that they will die, but the dead know nothing, and their memory is forgotten." The limit of death — consciousness ending, memory fading — is the condition Christ entered in his own death, and shattered in his resurrection. The dead who "know nothing" is not the NT's final word: "we will not all sleep, but we will all be changed" (1 Cor 15:51). Christ, who "died and came to life again" (Rev 1:18), is the first who having "known nothing" in death now knows all things.
9.6: The theme: their love, hatred, and envy have all vanished — the dead have no share in anything under the sun. The permanent severance of the dead from earthly participation defines the gravity of death — which is why Christ's resurrection, giving him "a share" again in the world (Luke 24:41–43, eating fish with the disciples after the resurrection), is so significant. His resurrection body shares in both worlds; his people will too (1 Cor 15:42–44).
9.7: The theme: "go, eat your food with gladness and drink your wine with a cheerful heart, for God has already approved what you do." This divine approval — already given — is remarkable: Qohelet commends present enjoyment as already-sanctioned by God. The NT grounds this approval specifically in Christ: those who are in Christ are accepted in the Beloved (Eph 1:6); God's approval is not in doubt for the one who is in Christ. The gladness commanded here is the gladness of those who know their standing before God is secure.
9.8: The theme: "let your clothing always be fresh and white, and your head not lack for oil" — the imagery of festive readiness, the well-dressed participant in a celebration. The NT's eschatological dimension takes this imagery and gives it weight: the white garments of the saints (Rev 7:9, 3:4) and the anointing of the Spirit (1 John 2:27; 2 Cor 1:21–22) are the permanent versions of the fresh clothing and oil that Qohelet commends for mortal days. Christ is the one who clothes his people in righteousness (Isa 61:10).
9.9: The theme: "enjoy life with the wife you love throughout all your fleeting days... for that is your portion." The commendation of marital love as one's portion under the sun is the created-good that Paul affirms as holy and undefiled when received with thanksgiving (1 Tim 4:3–4; Heb 13:4). The NT adds the eschatological frame: marriage is the shadow (Eph 5:31–32) of the permanent union between Christ and his church — the portion under the sun pointing forward to the eternal feast.
9.10: Direct: "Whatever your hand finds to do, do it with all your strength, for there is no work, no planning, no knowledge, no wisdom in the grave." Paul quotes this urgency in his own resurrection-grounded work: "Therefore, my beloved brothers and sisters, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain" (1 Cor 15:58). The resurrection transforms the urgency of Ecclesiastes 9:10: work with all your strength because there is no work in the grave — and the resurrection means the grave is not the end.
9.11: The theme: the race is not won by the swift, nor the battle by the strong — time and chance happen to all. This observation that human excellence is not determinative resonates with the NT's consistent subversion of human advantage: "God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong" (1 Cor 1:27). Christ himself does not win by conventional strength but through apparent weakness; the cross is the extreme case of "time and chance" overrunning apparent advantage — and being overrun in turn by resurrection.
9.12: The theme: no one knows when their hour will come — people are trapped at some evil moment when it falls suddenly upon them. Christ speaks of his own "hour" throughout John — "the hour has not yet come" — and when it arrives, it falls upon him suddenly in Gethsemane. Yet unlike those "trapped at some evil moment," he enters his hour willingly (John 10:18). He who walks into the trap that snares all becomes the one who springs it from within, setting the captives free (Heb 2:15).
9.13: The revelation of God: wisdom observed and found remarkable — the divine order that places wisdom at work in the world even when it is unrecognized. The Lord who gave wisdom to Solomon (1 Kgs 3) and whose wisdom runs through creation (Prov 8) leaves traces of himself even in Qohelet's observations. The remarkable wisdom in this example points toward the One who is wisdom personified (1 Cor 1:30).
9.14: The theme: the great siege of the small city by the powerful king — overwhelming force against a vulnerable community. The NT's corresponding image is the church in the world: "a city set on a hill" (Matt 5:14), besieged by the powers of darkness, yet unconquerable because "the gates of hell will not prevail against it" (Matt 16:18). The powerful king besieging the small city is the same pattern as the adversary who prowls around like a roaring lion (1 Pet 5:8).
9.15: A shadow: "a poor wise man was found in the city, and by his wisdom he saved it — yet no one remembered that poor man afterward." This is one of the clearest anticipatory shadows of Christ in Ecclesiastes. The structural correspondence: one poor man whose wisdom saves a community, but who is despised and forgotten. Isaiah 53:3 — "he was despised and rejected by men" — and Luke 4:24 — "a prophet is not accepted in his hometown" — take up this pattern. Christ saves the city (the people of God) through wisdom written in blood, and the one who was not remembered is now the name above every name (Phil 2:9). The shadow here is remarkably precise.
9.16: Direct: "wisdom is better than strength, but the poor man's wisdom is despised and his words go unheard." Paul cites this logic in 1 Corinthians 1:25: "the foolishness of God is wiser than human wisdom, and the weakness of God is stronger than human strength." The despised wisdom of the poor man is the type; Christ crucified — "to the Jews a stumbling block and to the Gentiles foolishness" (1 Cor 1:23) — is the fulfillment. The words that went unheard are now proclaimed to every nation.
9.17: The theme: words spoken quietly by the wise carry more weight than the shouting of a ruler among fools. Christ teaches with quiet authority — "not as the scribes" (Matt 7:29), without theatrical performance. The still, small voice (1 Kgs 19:12) of divine wisdom against the loud noise of earthly power is the pattern of Christ's own ministry: not with fanfare but in the back rooms and on hillsides, speaking words that outlasted every shouting ruler of his age.
9.18: The theme: wisdom is better than weapons of war, but one fool can undo a great deal of good. The fragility of wisdom in a world where one fool can unravel what wisdom built is the Ecclesiastes picture of history's instability. The NT's answer is not that wisdom becomes unbeatable by skill but that Christ's wisdom — established by resurrection — cannot be undone by any fool. The one who "destroyed the wisdom of the wise" (1 Cor 1:19) by raising the crucified Jesus has secured a wisdom that no fool can undo again.

1.1: A shadow: Qohelet, the Assembler/Preacher, speaks. The title qohelet derives from qahal (assembly, congregation) — the one who gathers or addresses the assembly. Christ is the ultimate Preacher-Teacher who gathers his assembly (Matt 5:1: he opened his mouth and taught; Luke 4:18-19: the Spirit of the Lord is upon me to preach). But where Qohelet gathers to deliver a verdict of vanity, Christ gathers to announce the kingdom — not 'all is hebel' but 'blessed are you.' The book's opening word signals the whole: a wisdom preacher addressing God's people about the nature of human existence.
1.2: A type: 'Vanity of vanities, all is vanity.' The Hebrew hevel (breath, vapor) is the diagnosis of the post-fall condition of creation — everything human striving builds dissolves like breath in cold air. Paul identifies this directly in Romans 8:20: 'the creation was subjected to futility [mataiotes, the LXX word for hevel] not willingly, but because of him who subjected it, in hope.' The hevel-condition is the consequence of the fall; Christ's work is the answer. The 'not in vain' of 1 Corinthians 15:58 — 'your labor in the Lord is not in vain' — is the direct reversal of 'all is vanity': the resurrection of Christ breaks the hevel-cycle. Qohelet's diagnosis is accurate; the cure is what he cannot yet see.
1.3: A theme: 'What does man gain [yitron] from all his toil at which he toils under the sun?' Yitron is a commercial term — surplus, profit left over after expenses. Qohelet's question is: what is the net gain of a human life? His answer will be 'nothing.' Paul answers from the other side of the resurrection: 'your labor in the Lord is not in vain' (1 Cor 15:58); 'to live is Christ, and to die is gain' (Phil 1:21). The yitron-question Qohelet asks, Christ answers by being himself the profit: in him, labor has eternal weight. The 'under the sun' qualifier matters — all toil within the merely earthly frame produces no lasting surplus. Christ breaks the frame.
1.4: A shadow: 'A generation goes, and a generation comes, but the earth remains forever.' The endless cycle of human generations without permanence — what one generation builds, the next inherits and then loses. Hebrews 1:10-12 addresses this directly, quoting Psalm 102 of Christ: 'You, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning, and the heavens are the work of your hands; they will perish, but you remain; they will all wear out like a garment... but you are the same, and your years will have no end.' The creation that 'remains forever' by Qohelet's observation does not actually remain — even it will perish. Only Christ remains unchanged through all the generational cycling.
1.5: A shadow: 'The sun rises, and the sun sets, and hastens to its place where it rises.' The sun's endless cycle — the image of relentless, purposeless repetition. John 8:12: 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.' The sun Qohelet observes cycling without purpose becomes in Christ the image of the true light who does not cycle into darkness: Revelation 22:5 — 'night will be no more. They will need no light of lamp or sun, for the Lord God will be their light.' The solar cycle is the creation-image for hevel; Christ is the light that ends the cycle.
1.6: A shadow: 'The wind goes toward the south and turns to the north; around and around goes the wind, and on its circuits the wind returns.' The wind's purposeless circling reinforces the vanity-of-cycles motif. But Christ uses the wind as the image of the Spirit's sovereign, purposive work: 'The wind blows where it wishes, and you hear its sound, but you do not know where it comes from or where it goes. So it is with everyone who is born of the Spirit' (John 3:8). The wind that Qohelet observes cycling in meaningless circuits is reframed by Christ as the image of the Spirit's free and regenerating movement — the same wind, but now purposive and life-giving rather than vanity-illustrating.
1.7: A shadow: 'All streams run to the sea, yet the sea is not full; to the place where the streams flow, there they flow again.' The unfillable sea — the image of unsatisfied longing, the human condition of desire that nothing satisfies. Revelation 22:1 presents the eschatological fulfillment: 'Then the angel showed me the river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb.' The sea that is never full in Qohelet's observation becomes in the new creation the river of life that flows from the Lamb's throne — not an endless unsatisfied cycle, but a perpetual outflow of life. John 4:14: 'the water that I will give him will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life.'
1.8: A direct connection: 'All things are full of weariness; a man cannot utter it; the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing.' The unsatisfied eye and ear — no earthly thing can fill the human capacity for seeing and hearing. Christ's answer is direct: 'I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst' (John 6:35). The eye not satisfied with seeing is satisfied when it sees Christ (John 14:9: 'Whoever has seen me has seen the Father'). The ear not filled with hearing is filled by his words (John 6:68: 'Lord, to whom shall we go? You have the words of eternal life'). What no earthly thing could fill, Christ fills.
1.9: A type: 'What has been is what will be, and what has been done is what will be done, and there is nothing new under the sun.' The no-novelty principle — history as endless recurrence with no new thing. 2 Corinthians 5:17: 'Therefore, if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come.' Christ is the absolute exception to the nothing-new principle: his resurrection is the first genuinely new thing since the creation — a body that has passed through death and emerged transformed, not merely resuscitated. The new creation is not a repetition of the old. Revelation 21:5: 'Behold, I am making all things new.' The thing Qohelet declares impossible — something genuinely new — is precisely what Christ brings.
1.10: A shadow: 'Is there a thing of which it is said, See, this is new? It has been already in the ages before us.' The skeptic's challenge to novelty claims — everything that seems new has already existed before. Christ's response to this entire trajectory is the resurrection, which is categorically unprecedented: no one in history had ever been raised with a glorified, deathless body. 1 Corinthians 15:20: 'But in fact Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep.' The firstfruits language signals absolute novelty: a first-of-its-kind event. The 'already been before us' claim is the very thing the resurrection refutes.
1.11: A shadow: 'There is no remembrance of former things, nor will there be any remembrance of things that are to come among those who come after.' The oblivion of human memory — nothing is permanently remembered, everything fades. Isaiah 43:18-19: 'Remember not the former things, nor consider the things of old. Behold, I am doing a new thing; now it springs forth, do you not perceive it?' And Revelation 21:4: 'He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away.' In the new creation, what passes away is not the memory of the righteous (Ps 112:6: 'the righteous will be remembered forever') but the former sufferings — a reversal of Qohelet's amnesia principle.
1.12: A shadow: 'I the Preacher have been king over Israel in Jerusalem.' Qohelet identifies himself as the royal wisdom-seeker. Christ is the ultimate royal wisdom-seeker — not an earthly king exploring wisdom, but Wisdom himself made flesh and becoming the king who has 'searched out by wisdom all that is done under heaven' (1:13), and whose verdict is not vanity but 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life' (John 14:6). The king-wisdom figure that Solomon represents is a type of Christ the king-Wisdom (1 Cor 1:24: 'Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God').
1.13: A theme: 'And I applied my heart to seek and to search out by wisdom all that is done under heaven. It is an unhappy business that God has given to the children of man to be busy with.' The wisdom-quest applied to all human activity — Qohelet's comprehensive investigation. Colossians 2:3: 'In Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.' What Qohelet sought by investigation, Christ is by nature: the one in whom all wisdom is hidden. The 'unhappy business' of wisdom-seeking under the sun becomes in Christ the joyful discovery that wisdom has come down and made itself accessible. John 14:6: 'I am the truth' — the search is over.
1.14: A theme: 'I have seen everything that is done under the sun, and behold, all is vanity and a striving after wind.' The conclusion of the wisdom-survey: all earthly achievement and knowledge produces only vanity. John 6:27: 'Do not work for the food that perishes, but for the food that endures to eternal life, which the Son of Man will give to you.' The labor that produces only vanity (striving after wind) is contrasted with labor directed toward Christ, who gives the food that endures. The shift from 'all is vanity' to 'labor in the Lord is not in vain' (1 Cor 15:58) depends entirely on whether the labor is 'under the sun' (earthly frame only) or 'in the Lord' (resurrection frame).
1.15: A type pointing to prophetic fulfillment: 'What is crooked cannot be made straight, and what is lacking cannot be counted.' Qohelet's statement of the limits of human wisdom — there are disorders that wisdom cannot correct. Isaiah 40:4 and Luke 3:5 announce the opposite: 'every valley shall be filled, and every mountain and hill shall be made low, and the crooked shall become straight, and the rough places shall become level ways.' The impossible that Qohelet declares — making crooked things straight — is precisely what John the Baptist announces as the work of Christ's coming. The theological claim of the gospel is that what human wisdom cannot accomplish, God's intervention through Christ can and will.
1.16: A shadow: 'I said in my heart, 'I have acquired great wisdom, surpassing all who were before me over Jerusalem, and my heart has had great experience of wisdom and knowledge.'' Solomon's claim to surpassing wisdom — the zenith of human wisdom achievement. Matthew 12:42: 'The queen of the South came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and behold, something greater than Solomon is here.' Christ's claim to be greater than Solomon — the very pinnacle Qohelet invokes — positions him as the one who stands beyond the entire wisdom-competition frame. What Solomon achieved was the maximum of human wisdom; Christ is divine wisdom in person (1 Cor 1:24).
1.17: A shadow: 'And I applied my heart to know wisdom and to know madness and folly. I perceived that this also is but a striving after wind.' Even the pursuit of wisdom itself turns out to be vanity — the ultimate irony of the wisdom-quest. 1 Corinthians 3:19: 'the wisdom of this world is folly with God.' Paul takes the Qohelet diagnosis and applies it theologically: earthly wisdom that does not reckon with God is foolishness. The inversion of Christ's cross becomes the new epistemology: 'the foolishness of God is wiser than men' (1 Cor 1:25). The wisdom Qohelet found to be striving after wind is precisely what Paul identifies as the world's pseudo-wisdom; the real wisdom is the crucified Christ.
1.18: A shadow: 'For in much wisdom is much vexation, and he who increases knowledge increases sorrow.' The wisdom-grief formula — the more Qohelet knows, the more he suffers from knowing. Isaiah 53:3: 'He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief.' Christ, the fullness of wisdom and knowledge (Col 2:3), is also the man of sorrows — the one who, knowing everything, bore the maximum of grief. The Qohelet formula points to the incarnation: to enter fully into human wisdom is to enter fully into human grief, which is exactly what the Son of God did. The wisdom-sorrow that Qohelet observes is fulfilled in the one who is both the fullness of wisdom and the bearer of human sorrow.

2.1: A shadow: 'I said in my heart, 'Come now, I will test you with pleasure; enjoy yourself.' But behold, this also was vanity.' The self-experiment with pleasure — testing whether enjoyment can answer the vanity-question. Hebrews 4:15: 'we have a high priest who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin.' Christ underwent the full range of human temptation (including the temptation to satisfy himself through pleasure, cf. Luke 4:1-13) without falling. Where Qohelet discovers that pleasure is vanity, Christ demonstrates that the proper response to temptation is not indulgence but faithfulness — 'man shall not live by bread alone' (Luke 4:4).
2.2: A shadow: 'I said of laughter, 'It is mad,' and of pleasure, 'What use is it?'' Qohelet's dismissal of laughter as madness and pleasure as purposeless. Luke 6:25: 'Woe to you who laugh now, for you will mourn and weep.' Christ's woe to hollow laughter stands with Qohelet's verdict. But the NT also promises the inversion: John 16:22: 'you will be sorrowful, but your sorrow will turn into joy... your joy no one will take from you.' The empty laughter Qohelet dismisses contrasts with the full joy Christ gives — not the laughter that is mad but the joy that is solid and permanent.
2.3: A shadow: 'I searched with my heart how to cheer my body with wine — my heart still guiding me with wisdom — and how to lay hold on folly, till I might see what was good for the children of man to do under heaven during the few days of their life.' Qohelet's search for legitimate pleasure through wine. John 2:1-11: Christ transforms water into wine at the wedding — not ending the pleasure of wine but providing better wine, a sign of the kingdom's abundance. But more: John 6:53-56 reframes the search entirely — the flesh and blood of Christ are the true food and drink, the thing Qohelet was searching for when he reached for wine. The experiment with wine points toward the one who gives wine as a sign of his blood poured out for the joy of the world.
2.4: A shadow: 'I made great works. I built houses and planted vineyards for myself.' Solomon as the great builder — the human capacity for construction pushed to its maximum. The ultimate Builder is Christ, who builds what will not fail: 'I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it' (Matt 16:18). And the eschatological construction project: Revelation 21:1-2 — the new Jerusalem coming down from God, prepared as a bride. The city that Qohelet builds on earth and then leaves behind becomes in Christ the city that descends from heaven and endures forever.
2.5: A shadow: 'I made myself gardens and parks, and planted in them all kinds of fruit trees.' The garden-paradise that Solomon constructs — the attempt to recreate Eden through human ingenuity. The trajectory of Scripture runs: Eden lost (Gen 3) → wilderness (Sinai) → promised land → the perfect garden of Song of Solomon 4:12-16 → the new creation's garden-city (Rev 22:1-5). Christ is the one who reopens the gate of paradise: 'today you will be with me in paradise' (Luke 23:43).
